papers.reason: Native closed-community I: Kim & Lim (CSCW 2015) 'Dwelling Places in KakaoTalk' ethnography of KakaoTalk chatrooms. Although KakaoTalk is a super-app at the platform level, the paper's unit of analysis is bounded chatrooms as closed functional regions (primary/secondary/tertiary) — exactly the closed-scope sociotechnical framing CampusRide needs (not mass-market business-model). HCI/CSCW venue. Supports paper_outline_v4.2 §2.2 Beat 2 ¶3 (integrated design space with bounded scope).

Native bounded-scope I: Ohashi, Kato & Hjorth (Social Media + Society 2017) 'Digital Genealogies: LINE in Japanese Families'. LINE is a super-app, but this paper studies LINE as integrated infrastructure inside the closed boundary of a family unit — bounded-scope community use of an integrated platform, not mass-market super-app behaviour. Directly parallels CampusRide's closed-campus-community integration logic. Supports §2.2 Beat 2 ¶3.

Native closed-community I: Wang, Kale & O'Neill (CHI 2020) 'Please Call the Specialism: Using WeChat to Support Patient Care in China'. Studies nurse-facilitated patient groups inside WeChat as a closed clinic-scoped coordination infrastructure — not WeChat as mass-market super-app but as bounded integrated platform for a professional community. CHI venue, HCI native. Supports §2.2 Beat 2 ¶3 integrated-platform-with-closed-scope argument.

Theoretical backbone for I: Steinberg (Social Media + Society 2020) 'LINE as Super App: Platformization in East Asia'. Serves as platform-studies / sociotechnical framing of super-app integrated design (direction 4 in Prompt B). NOT used as closed-community evidence — used as theoretical reference on super-app platformization and boundary resource logic, so CampusRide's closed-community scope can be positioned against this theoretical backdrop. Rationale explicitly flags this as theoretical framing, not empirical closed-community proxy. Supports §2.2 Beat 2 ¶3 theoretical foundation only.

Native E anchor: Ellison, Steinfield & Lampe (2007, JCMC) study Facebook use by Michigan State undergraduates during the era when Facebook required a .edu email to join, making institutional identity the platform's defining gating primitive. Cited as baseline empirical evidence that .edu-scoped closed-community SNS lowers stranger-coordination costs and builds bridging social capital among college students. Supports paper outline v4.2 §2.3 Beat 3 ¶1 Identity primitive anchor — the foundational .edu-gated SNS finding. Manual inclusion: E category automated retrieval returned 0 native papers.

Native E: Ellison, Gray, Lampe & Fiore (2014, New Media & Society) analyze resource-mobilization requests on Facebook, whose network structure was seeded by .edu-gated college cohorts. The paper foregrounds how institutionally-seeded ties enable favor/help requests — the mechanism CampusRide's identity primitive aims to reproduce in a six-module campus platform. Supports paper outline v4.2 §2.3 Beat 3 ¶1 Identity primitive: evidence that institutional-identity-seeded closed-community graphs yield mobilizable bonding + bridging capital, distinct from generic sharing-economy identity verification. Manual inclusion: E category automated retrieval returned 0 native papers.

Native E: Schlesinger, Chandrasekharan, Masden, Bruckman, Edwards & Grinter (2017, CHI) interview study of 18 Yik Yak users on a single urban American university campus, analyzing how hyper-locality (the app's geofence to one campus) plus anonymity plus ephemerality shape community identity. The hyper-locality primitive is a functional cousin of .edu-email gating — both bind platform membership to one institution's geography — and this is the canonical HCI treatment of campus-bounded anonymous platforms. Supports paper outline v4.2 §2.3 Beat 3 ¶1 Identity primitive: campus-bounded identity as trust primitive, not as generic sharing-economy identity proxy. Manual inclusion: E category automated retrieval returned 0 native papers.

Native E: Black, Mezzina & Thompson (2016, Computers in Human Behavior) content analysis of 4,000 Yaks sampled from 42 US university campuses — the largest empirical corpus on what happens inside campus-bounded anonymous platforms. Directly relevant to the E primitive: the campus geofence functions as institutional identity scoping in the absence of .edu email credential. Supports paper outline v4.2 §2.3 Beat 3 ¶1 Identity primitive — cross-campus empirical baseline for what campus-scoping primitive actually produces behaviourally. Manual inclusion: E category automated retrieval returned 0 native papers.

Native E: Heston & Birnholtz (2016, iConference) study Yik Yak through the SIDE (social identity model of deindividuation effects) framework, focusing on how location-based filtering manufactures a bounded campus identity even without explicit .edu credential. A direct theoretical treatment of institutional/spatial identity as trust primitive in a campus-bounded platform — exactly the native-E case the paper outline's Identity primitive paragraph needs. Supports paper outline v4.2 §2.3 Beat 3 ¶1 Identity primitive anchor. Manual inclusion: E category automated retrieval returned 0 native papers.

Native E: Wu, Minkus & Ross (2017, ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology) analyze location-based anonymous mobile apps (Yik Yak, Whisper) across US college campuses, measuring how campus-scoped anonymity produces discoverable but bounded content pools. Demonstrates the measurable behavioral footprint of the campus-identity primitive and characterizes its privacy/identity trade-offs — a native-E design-science treatment, not a generic sharing-economy identity proxy. Supports paper outline v4.2 §2.3 Beat 3 ¶1 Identity primitive. Manual inclusion: E category automated retrieval returned 0 native papers.

Supports claim X1 (rating sensitivity differs between active drivers and passive raters — asymmetric). Fradkin, Grewal & Holtz (Marketing Science 2021) 'Reciprocity and Unveiling in Two-Sided Reputation Systems' run a field experiment on Airbnb where feedback was hidden until both parties submitted a review (treatment) vs revealed immediately (control). The treatment reduced retaliation/reciprocation and lowered ratings — direct causal evidence that the two sides of a peer marketplace behave asymmetrically toward the rating system, and that architectural choices around reveal-timing change that asymmetry. Cited in §4.2 Beat 5 ¶3 to anchor the X1 claim that active drivers and passive raters are not symmetric rating actors. RATIONALE NOTE: econ/marketplace framing of 'fairness' differs from HCI framing — we cite only for the asymmetry mechanism, not for normative fairness claims. Also feeds §5.8.3 design decision on reveal-window / dispute window. Manual inclusion: H-subgroup corpus for Beat 5 driver-subset finding (Prompt E, Direction 1).

Supports claim X2 (amateur / occasional drivers — not professional gig workers — show rating anxiety). Neifer, Bossauer, Pakusch, Boehm & Lawo (CSCW 2023) 'Trust-Building in Peer-to-Peer Carsharing: Design Case Study for Algorithm-Based Reputation Systems' study Turo/Getaround-style platforms where private car owners (amateurs who share their own car, not full-time gig workers) grapple with biased/fake ratings and algorithmic scoring. 16 problem-centered interviews + 12-participant evaluation of an algorithmic-score app. This is a native HCI/CSCW treatment of amateur peer-driver rating experience and explicitly distinguishes P2P amateur carsharing from commercial ride-hailing labor. Directly supports §4.2 Beat 5 ¶3 claim that rating anxiety is not confined to professional gig labor, and the §5.8.3 rating-system-with-fairness-consideration design. RATIONALE NOTE: explicitly NOT a Rosenblat/Stark or Lee-style algorithmic management paper — participants are private owners, not professional drivers. Manual inclusion: H-subgroup corpus, Prompt E Direction 2.

Supports claim X2 (amateur / occasional drivers show rating anxiety in peer-to-peer carpool settings). Hartl, Penz & Schuessler (Journal of Cleaner Production 2025) 'Creating a Trusting Environment in the Sharing Economy: Unpacking Mechanisms for Trust-Building Used by Peer-to-Peer Carpooling Platforms' qualitatively compare BlaBlaCar, Oszkar, Zego and Carpul on review systems, GPS-tracking, offline events and key partners, then run a laboratory experiment (N=163) showing that trust-building mechanisms (including review/rating systems) have different consequences for car owners vs non-car-owners and for more vs less experienced users. This is the closest native literature we have to amateur / non-professional driver rating experience, and the owner-vs-non-owner split parallels our Driver/Both vs Rider-only subset split. Supports §4.2 Beat 5 ¶3 and acts as partial literature precedent for the amateur-driver extension. RATIONALE NOTE: not HCI venue (Journal of Cleaner Production), but direct P2P carpool empirical study, not professional gig labor. Manual inclusion: H-subgroup corpus, Prompt E Direction 2.

Supports claim X3 / defensive scoping for small-sample rating methodology. Caine (CHI 2016) 'Local Standards for Sample Size at CHI' systematically analyzes all CHI 2014 papers and documents that sample sizes range 1–916,000 with a modal value of N=12 and distributions differing by study type. This provides a citable 'local standard' defense for reporting a descriptive rating finding on N=19 (driver subset) — it establishes that CHI accepts small-sample descriptive studies and contextualizes our §4.2 Beat 5 ¶2 methodological justification and §7.2 Beat 7 ¶2 sample-skew acknowledgement. Directly cited in §4.2 ¶2 (subset justification) and §7.2 ¶2 (sample skew). Manual inclusion: H-subgroup corpus, Prompt E Direction 3 (methodology defense).

primary_anchors.title: Cornell Carpool System Survey (Ruochong et al., 2026)
primary_anchors.abstract: Formative survey of Cornell / Ithaca area students on campus carpooling needs, willingness-to-pay for safety and identity features, driver-side tolerance for passenger behavior, and motivational structure. N=117 launched, 50 fully completed; 82% Mandarin-native; descriptive statistics only.
primary_anchors.note_for_pipeline: Primary-data shim for Beats 4 and 5. Not to be retrieved as a paper; acts as a structural anchor in narrative_chains for these beats.

primary_anchors.title: CampusRide Multi-Module Platform Technical Documentation
primary_anchors.abstract: System reference for the CampusRide platform: Vue 3 + Express + Supabase + Socket.IO stack. Six modules (carpool, marketplace, activities, groups, messaging, points) share four design primitives: institutional identity verification, safety, rating fairness, and gamification. Carpool module is developed in depth.
primary_anchors.note_for_pipeline: Artifact shim for Beat 6. Not to be retrieved as a paper; acts as a structural anchor in narrative_chains for this beat.

primary_anchors.title: Research-Agent: A Thesis-Conditioned Evidence Pipeline
primary_anchors.abstract: Eight-phase evidence pipeline (corpus assembly, classification, deep extraction, relationship graph + evidence sufficiency, narrative chains, contradiction map, evidence inventory, five-reviewer evaluation) supporting thesis-conditioned literature synthesis. Contribution 3 of the paper.
primary_anchors.note_for_pipeline: Methodology reference for §3.2 and §6. Phase 5 reviewers should evaluate how the paper uses the pipeline, not the pipeline itself.

_note: primary_anchors hold the Beat 4 / Beat 6 pseudo-anchors needed because Beats 4 and 6 are primary_data/artifact beats that have no real literature anchor. phase3_5_narrative.py reads these when building narrative_chains for those beats; phase_contracts.ensure_narrative_chains_valid allows paperId to be 'local:' prefixed for primary_data/artifact beats.
